items.correct: Holding minimal stock and receiving inputs only when needed for production — reducing storage costs but increasing vulnerability to supply disruption.
items.misconception: Just-in-time means delivering products to customers exactly on time.
items.random_wrong: Just-in-time means finishing work right before the deadline.
items.why: JIT is about INPUT stock to production, not output delivery to customers. Students misread the term as a delivery promise; it is an internal stock control philosophy.

items.correct: Shareholders' personal assets are protected if the company fails — they can only lose the amount they invested, not their personal wealth.
items.misconception: Limited liability means the company has limited debts it can take on.
items.random_wrong: Limited liability means a business can only operate for a limited time.
items.why: Limited liability is about the LIMIT on what shareholders can lose, not on what the company can do. Students misread the term as restricting the business rather than protecting investors.

items.q: How does HRM differ from traditional personnel management?
items.correct: HRM treats people as a strategic asset and integrates human resource policies with business strategy; personnel management is more administrative and reactive, handling hiring, pay and disputes.
items.misconception: HRM and personnel are exactly the same thing — just a name change.
items.random_wrong: HRM only applies to large multinational companies.
items.why: The shift from personnel to HRM is conceptual — from administrative function to strategic partner. Students treat the relabel as cosmetic, missing the strategic management literature behind it.

items.q: Clayton Christensen's 'The Innovator's Dilemma' was published in:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1997. “1985” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Number of steps in Kotter's change management model?
items.why: Exact answer: 8 steps. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
